summary:
In observational studies like this one, recall bias may lead to inaccurate self-reporting of exposures—such as folic acid intake—and thus potentially mislead conclusions about its link to autism risk due to systematic errors in how participants report their pre-pregnancy habits.

The summary provided by Agent2 correctly identifies that retrospective surveys are prone to recall bias when assessing maternal behavior prior to conception; specifically, women whose children do not have ASD may be more likely to accurately remember—or falsely believe they took folic acid—which introduces selection error into outcome-based comparisons. This undermines confidence in any inferred relationship between folic acid supplementation and autism development because it distorts true exposure-disease relationships through flawed data collection methods inherent in subjective recollection.

This undermines confidence in any inferred relationship between folic acid supplementation and autism development because it distorts true exposure-disease relationships through flawed data collection methods inherent in subjective recollection.. Answer choice: C
